text: Hey you, yeah you. Do you want to write
a fantasy show? Then here's a crazy idea
for you. Start out with a likable,
good-natured protagonist who comes from
humble origins and send him on a journey
into the wider world for the first time.
Along the way, he meets friends and
enemies, encounters challenges and
setbacks, and has to make some tough
decisions that give the audience a
better insight into who he is. But with
a little help from his friends and some
good old-fashioned grit and
determination, he manages to stay true
to his principles and wins through to
the end, becoming a stronger and wiser
man as a result. Like I say, it's a
crazy idea in the post-modern Hollywood
landscape where heroism, bravery,
masculine determination, and optimism
seem to have been outlawed, but holy
[ __ ] is it working for A Night of the
Seven Kingdoms. Probably the best show
on TV right now and fast becoming my
favorite show in the Game of Thrones
universe. No way. Yes way.
>> I was going to wait until the season
finale next week to deliver my final
verdict on this one, but unless the main
character suddenly starts eating babies,
I'm pretty confident in saying that A
Night of the Seven Kingdoms is [ __ ]
excellent. Why? Well, it's not because
of the epic scale or the record-breaking
budget, the sprawling cast of
characters, or the mind-blowing special
effects, because it doesn't have any of
those things. It's a smaller, more
modest kind of show that knows exactly
what it is, that has no aspirations to
be anything bigger or grander, and
instead just sets out to do the best it
can with what it has. It's one of those
wonderful examples of resource
constraints actually working in a
production's favor, forcing the
showrunners to rely less on scale and
spectacle and more on good writing and
characters. Because guess what? It
doesn't need anything else. When people
are invested in your story, they'll
forgive all manner of technical
shortcomings. I mean, have you seen the
[ __ ] we used to watch in the '9s and
2000s? Trust me, we weren't there for
the special effects. But if not, then no
amount of shallow visual spectacle is
going to change that. Anyway, before I
plunge any deeper into my analysis,
allow me to furnish you with a plot
summary. The story centers around Sir
Duncan the Tall, a man who will one day
go on to become a legendary figure in
Game of Thrones lore.
>> Sir Duncan the Tall. Four pages for Sir
Duncan. He must have been quite a man,
>> so they say.
>> But when we first meet him, he's nothing
but a lowly squire to an obscure hedge
knight who's just passed away. After
burying the old man and taking up his
sword, Dunc decides to enter a local
jousting tournament with the vague goal
of winning some respect as a knight and
maybe even joining the king's guard
someday. A modest quest for a modest
man, but straight away you absolutely
want him to succeed. Partly because of
the sharp writing that immediately
fleshes him out as a simple but decent
man. not especially smart or cunning,
but with a good heart and a desire to
become more than he is, and partly
because of a great performance from lead
actor Peter Clafy, who just radiates
likability. Now, this mission isn't as
easy as it sounds because his former
master wasn't exactly a rock star as far
as knights go. None of the noblemen that
he approaches have ever heard of the
guy, and if Dunc can't prove his
lineage, then he can't enter the
tournament. While he searches for a
solution, he also strikes up a
friendship with a mysterious young boy
with a shaved head who identifies
himself only as Egg because his head
looks like a [ __ ] egg, I guess, and
offers to be Dunk Squire. As it turns
out, though, help arrives from the most
unlikely of places in the form of Prince
Bor Targaryen, a conscientious man who
makes a point of remembering even the
most obscure hedge knights that served
under him. He vouches for Dunk, allowing
him to enter the tournament. Great
stuff. Unfortunately, it all goes a bit
wrong when Dunc gets into a fight with
Bor's nephew, Aaron, to defend the honor
of a young woman. Aon's pissed with him
and demands trial by combat, but with a
twist. Instead of a one-on-one fight,
it'll be Dunc and six other men against
seven chosen by Aeron. But what will
happen in this medieval royal rumble?
Who will live and who will die? And why
do I have a sudden craving for a bacon
and egg sandwich? You know, it's been
kind of fascinating watching the change
in attitude towards this show over the
past several weeks. When it was first
announced, I don't think many people
were exactly burning with anticipation
for it. Game of Thrones had ended on a
bum note that soured millions of people
on Westeros forever, and House of the
Dragon, for all its capable writing and
strong cast, had never managed to
recapture the same magic. That show was
a lot of things, but fun wasn't one of
them. And yet, almost from the first
episode, people seemed to perk right up
and opinions started to change. And that
sentiment's only gotten stronger with
each episodes. There was this weird
ground swell of positivity and for lack
of a better word, relief. Relief that we
weren't being subjected to yet another
season of grim, dark misery porn with a
reluctant fantasy veneer. Relief that
for once we had a genuinely brave,
likable, decent protagonist to cheer for
who wasn't harboring some dark secret or
machavellian scheme to destroy
everything and everyone around him.
Relief that we finally got a sincere
story that explored concepts like honor
and heroism without trying to mock or
belittle them as outdated relics from a
different age. Relief that we even get
to see men in full battle armor beating
the absolute [ __ ] out of each other
without the addition of some [ __ ]
ridiculous 100b girl boss pretending
like she can fight just as well as the
guys.
>> Girls get it done. After a decade of
Hollywood's self-imposed malaise,
depressing moral ambiguity that inspires
absolutely nobody, and tedious culture
war panderin that pisses off everybody,
this show honestly feels like the breath
of fresh air that everyone was
desperately ready for. Even now watching
it, I kept asking myself, how did they
get away with this in 2026? How did they
avoid all the tedious tropes that we've
been told were essential for a modern
audience?
I don't know, man. Either this show was
lowprofile enough and lucky enough to
fly under the radar, or and this is a
much more exciting possibility, maybe
Hollywood are finally realizing what
their audience wants and more
importantly what they don't want.
Because here's the thing, we are [ __ ]
done with the bleak nihilism where
everybody is bad and nothing's worth
fighting for. We're done with the
morally gray protagonists that nobody
really likes. We're done with the ironic
mockery and the sneering post-modern
criticisms of classic heroic archetypes.
We're done with the girl bosses and the
diversity quotas and the alphabet soup
of sexualities that have to get crammed
into absolutely everything. They don't.
We don't need any of it. What we really
need is a bit of optimism again, a bit
of good old-fashioned adventure, a
window into another world that's
actually exciting and interesting to
explore. And that's exactly what A Night
of the Seven Kingdoms delivers from a
perspective that you don't often see.
We're used to seeing Westeros from the
point of view of kings and generals and
epic power struggles in palaces and
fortresses. It's kind of rare to view
that world through the lens of the
common man through small villages and
farms and minor tournaments in far-flung
provincial outposts. There's a charming
little slice of life aspect to the show
that I think it's important not to
overlook. Narratively, it never
doawdles, but it also takes just enough
time to let us tune into the world to
get a feel for it and the people who
live there. And because it's not weighed
down by a hundred different plot threads
and characters that it has to keep
jumping between, for once, it allows us
to just settle in and bond with our
leads. Speaking of which, Dunc and Egg
make for a great pairing. Contradictory,
but also complimentary. One's physically
powerful, but naive and inexperienced
with the wider world. The other is small
and puny, but deceptively wise, and way
more connected than he lets on. The two
actors have got superb chemistry
together and some of the most enjoyable
scenes of the show are just the two of
them hanging out, talking together,
sharing a meal. It's simple stuff, but
you'd be amazed how effective simple
things like this can be when you've been
starved of them for years. Honestly, I
just can't say enough good things about
A Night of the Seven Kingdoms. It's a
show that put fun and adventure back
into fantasy again that actually makes
me feel good when I'm watching it
instead of despairing over the human
condition. and it's one of the few shows
on TV that I actually look forward to
each week. Is it a lucky fluke that
somehow made it through the net? Or does
it signal the start of a major
realignment in Hollywood's creative
output? I don't know yet, but for now,
I'll [ __ ] take it.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
